questions.prompt: What is the main job of pom.xml in a Maven project?
options: It stores the test results after a run
It declares dependencies so Maven downloads them and puts them on the classpath
It lists which browsers to install
It replaces the need for TestNG
questions.explanation: pom.xml is where you declare dependencies like Selenium and TestNG. Maven reads it, fetches the exact versions, and makes them available — no manual jar wrangling.

questions.prompt: Where does Maven's standard layout expect test code to live?
options: the project root next to pom.xml
anywhere, Maven scans the whole disk
questions.explanation: Maven's convention puts production code in src/main/java and tests in src/test/java. Following it means Maven and the IDE locate everything without extra configuration.

questions.prompt: What does a testng.xml file let you control?
options: The CSS styling of the test report
Which classes, packages, or groups of tests to run as a suite
The version of Selenium that downloads
questions.explanation: testng.xml defines a suite: which classes or packages run, which groups to include, and any parameters — so you can run a chosen subset instead of every test.

questions.prompt: You tag fast checks with @Test(groups = "smoke"). What does this enable?
options: Running only the smoke group for a quick check, separate from the full regression run
Forcing every test to run twice
questions.explanation: Groups are labels on tests. testng.xml can include just the 'smoke' group, letting you run a fast subset on every push while reserving the full suite for a complete regression run.
